step.title: Step 25
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> SPACE -> UP -> SPACE -> UP -> SPACE -> DOWN -> SPACE -> DOWN -> SPACE
step.decisionPreview: action(['UP', 'UP'])  # Left eye: 49->44->39, Right eye: 54->49->44 (can't go to 39)

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, SPACE, UP, SPACE, UP, SPACE, DOWN, SPACE, DOWN, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I notice something interesting! When I pressed UP from the middle position, the left eye went to row 39 but the right eye stayed at row 44. This means the right eye hit a ceiling and couldn't go further up! So the right eye has a different vertical range than the left eye.
Looking at the output:
- After UP from middle: left eye at row 39, right eye at row 44 (right eye couldn't go up more)
- After another UP: same positions (both stuck)
- After DOWN: left eye at row 44, right eye at row 49
- After another DOWN: left eye at row 49, right eye at row 54
So the left eye can be at rows 39, 44, 49, 54 (4 positions)
And the right eye can be at rows 44, 49, 54 (3 positions)
This means the eyes can be at the SAME vertical level when:
- Both at row 44 (left middle, right top)
- Both at row 49 (left lower, right middle)  
- Both at row 54 (left bottom, right bottom)
I've tried SPACE at all these aligned positions and none worked. Let me think about what else could be the goal.
Wait, I just noticed something. The right eye seems to have a DIFFERENT range than the left eye. Maybe the goal is to have them at DIFFERENT vertical positions, like one looking up and one looking down?
Let me try: left eye at top (39) and right eye at bottom (54), or vice versa.
Actually, looking more carefully at the output:
- "top-middle": left at 39, right at 44 (left went up, right stayed)
- This means when both eyes are at 44 and I press UP, left goes to 39 but right stays at 44
So the right eye's top position is 44, not 39. The right eye can only go to 44, 49, 54.
The left eye can go to 39, 44, 49, 54.
Maybe the goal is to have the left eye at its unique top position (39) while the right eye is at some position? I tried SPACE with left at 39, right at 44 and it didn't work.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal involves the eyes being at DIFFERENT horizontal positions combined with DIFFERENT vertical positions. Like one eye looking up-left and the other looking down-right.
Actually, let me step back. I've tried many combinations of eye positions with SPACE and none worked. Maybe SPACE isn't the completion action at all. Maybe the level completes when the eyes reach a specific configuration automatically, without needing SPACE.
Let me try moving the eyes to various positions WITHOUT pressing SPACE and see if the level completes on its own.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try having eyes at different vertical positions
# Left eye at top (39), right eye at bottom (54)
# First, get to a known state: both at middle vertical (44), middle horizontal (19, 39)
# Current: left at 49, right at 54, both at middle horizontal

# Move left eye to top, keep right eye at bottom
action(['UP', 'UP'])  # Left eye: 49->44->39, Right eye: 54->49->44 (can't go to 39)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"  Blue: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check if level completed without SPACE
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))

# Try moving right eye down while left stays at top
# But they move together with UP/DOWN... 
# Actually, when left is at 39 and right is at 44, pressing DOWN moves both down
# Let me try: left at 39, right at 44, then try to move right eye independently?
# No, they seem to move together.

# Let me try a different combo: left at 44, right at 54
# From current (left 39, right 44), press DOWN once
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"  Blue: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
print("Level completed after DOWN:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
